blocks.title: Overview
blocks.text:
Knowing a framework and applying it to your own life are two different things. This program treats that gap as a design problem, not a motivation problem. Across the sessions that follow, learners will use structured tools — behavioral diagnostics, guided reflection prompts, schedule audits, and interactive exercises matched to each chapter's demands — to move from concept to personal inventory.

Read Before You Listen: This chapter introduces the animating logic of the entire course — that self-knowledge is not a preliminary step before leadership development begins, but is itself the primary work. The session audio and slides will demonstrate how the three-fold purpose framework operates as a diagnostic rather than a destination. As you listen, you are tracking structure: how the framework's three questions sequence, why that sequence matters, and where participants most commonly stall. The text you are reading now is designed to stand on its own if the audio is unavailable; every framework element introduced in the recording is named and defined here.

The central construct in this opening session is ruthless self-knowledge — a phrase that needs unpacking before it becomes useful. In the DVULI context, ruthless does not mean harsh or self-critical in a therapeutic sense. It means disciplined refusal to substitute roles for identity. Leaders — particularly those in ministry, nonprofit, or community development contexts — frequently know what they do with precision and know why they do it in the abstract. What they carry into this program, more often, is a blurred account of who they are underneath those roles. Ruthless self-knowledge is the practice of separating those layers: distinguishing the gifts you carry from the positions you occupy, the values that actually govern your decisions from the values you endorse verbally, and the design you were made for from the trajectory you have drifted into. The three-fold purpose framework from What Color Is Your Parachute? structures that separation into workable questions rather than leaving it as an open-ended reflection.

To see why the distinction matters in practice, consider what happens when it breaks down. A young pastor moves from a thriving mentorship role into a senior leadership position — a change that looks like advancement but requires a fundamentally different behavioral profile. He is highly relational, processes decisions through conversation, and draws energy from one-on-one work. The new role demands administrative execution, platform presence, and comfort with ambiguity at scale. He is not unqualified, but he is misaligned, and because he has never mapped his actual gifts against his role requirements, he interprets the friction as a faith problem rather than a design problem. He prays harder. He pushes through. Two years later he is exhausted, his closest relationships in the congregation are strained, and he cannot explain why. The tools introduced across this course — beginning with the three-fold framework in this session — are built to surface that kind of misalignment before it compounds, and to give it precise language so it can be addressed rather than endured.

The interactive layer is intentionally varied. Chapters covering behavioral profiling, scenario planning, and systems thinking use formats built for those tasks: branching vignettes where you assign and defend a DISC read, drag-to-configure simulations that let you weight uncertainty and impact variables, and causal-loop mapping exercises that ask you to connect nodes before comparing your construction to an expert model. Chapters covering more bounded concepts use low-stakes self-assessments — multiple-select or ranked-choice questions — positioned before explanation rather than after, to activate what you already carry into the material before the framework challenges it.

Each chapter also closes with a short set of practice questions available through the course LMS. These unlock 48 hours after the corresponding session and remain open until the next class meets, giving you a defined window to test your retention while the material is still recent but no longer in front of you. These are not graded gatekeepers; they are consolidation checkpoints designed to surface what you actually retained versus what you only recognized while reading. Some questions ask you to identify — which DISC style, which LSA step, which Core Values marker. Others ask you to apply: given a real leadership scenario, what does the framework actually say to do? Both types are tied directly to DVULI framework language, and each carries immediate answer feedback keyed to that terminology, so you can locate a conceptual gap precisely rather than simply learning you missed something.

Participants leave this opening session having completed the three-fold purpose framework from What Color Is Your Parachute?: to know God and enjoy Him forever; to make the world a better place; and to use their greatest gift in the place God has designed for them. What comes next is the work of finding out, specifically, what that means for you.

Session 1

Who Am I? The Biblical Case for Ruthless Self-Knowledge

Discovering your divine design begins with the most uncomfortable — and most transformative — question a leader can ask.

She had led a youth ministry for eleven years. She had seen young people walk away from gangs and walk toward diplomas. She had lost volunteers, buried teenagers, wept in church parking lots at midnight, and preached hope when she felt none. She had given everything she had. And then, on the first morning of the DeVos Urban Leadership Initiative's Local Workshop 1, a facilitator asked a question that stopped her cold.

"Don't tell me what you do," the facilitator said. "Tell us — who are you?"

The room went quiet. She opened her mouth and closed it again. In eleven years of ministry, no one had ever asked her that. More unsettling still: she realized, sitting in that circle, that she did not have a ready answer. She knew her program. She knew her outcomes. She knew her board members' names and her at-risk youth's phone numbers. But who she was — her strengths, her blind spots, the patterns of behavior that shaped every room she walked into — that knowledge was thin. She had been so busy doing the work that she had never stopped long enough to understand the worker.

This session is your formal invitation to stop. To look clearly — not more highly than you ought, but not less honestly than you should — at the person God has created you to be. What you discover in this space will form the root system for everything else DVULI is designed to grow in you.

The Invitation: Why Self-Knowledge Is Not Optional

Leadership development programs that skip the interior work — that jump straight to strategies, competencies, and skill-building — are building on sand. The most sophisticated tool in the hands of a leader who does not know himself or herself will, sooner or later, become an instrument of harm rather than help. This is not a harsh verdict; it is an observed reality. Burn-out, isolation, and leadership failure in urban ministry contexts are almost never primarily the result of insufficient technical skills. They are, more often, the result of leaders who never developed an honest, grounded, and faith-integrated account of who they are (Avolio, 2005).

The DVULI program calls the destination of this interior work divine design — the process of understanding who God has created you to be and what He has called and equipped you to do. But divine design cannot be found by looking outward alone, toward programs and plans and community needs. It must begin with the kind of inward honest reckoning that the Apostle Paul calls sober judgment — a phrase we will unpack carefully in the pages that follow.

Researcher Tasha Eurich's multi-year investigation into self-awareness found that only 10 to 15 percent of people actually possess the quality they claim. Most people, her research concludes, dramatically overestimate how clearly they see themselves. The consequences of this gap are not trivial: leaders with low self-awareness consistently make poorer decisions, build weaker teams, and cause more relational damage — usually while remaining genuinely convinced that the problem lies elsewhere (Eurich, 2017). The invitation to honest self-knowledge is, therefore, not an academic exercise. It is a prerequisite for sustainable, healthy, effective ministry leadership.

Pillar One: The Biblical Foundation for Self-Assessment

The DVULI program grounds its entire approach to personal assessment in two passages of Scripture that are worth reading slowly and carefully, because they are doing more work than they might initially appear to do.

"Carry each other's burdens, and in this way you will fulfill the law of Christ. If anyone thinks they are something when they are not, they deceive themselves. Each one should test their own actions. Then they can take pride in themselves alone, without comparing themselves to someone else, for each one should carry their own load." — Galatians 6:2–5 (NIV)

"Do not conform to the pattern of this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind. Then you will be able to test and approve what God's will is — his good, pleasing and perfect will. For by the grace given me I say to every one of you: Do not think of yourself more highly than you ought, but rather think of yourself with sober judgment, in accordance with the faith God has distributed to each of you. For just as each of us has one body with many members, and these members do not all have the same function, so in Christ we, though many, form one body, and each member belongs to all the others." — Romans 12:1–5 (NIV)

The first passage, from Paul's letter to the Galatians, establishes a crucial paradox at the heart of Christian community: you are simultaneously called to carry each other's burdens and to carry your own load. This is not a contradiction — it is a precise description of mature, differentiated community life. You cannot be a genuine burden-bearer for others unless you first know clearly what your own load looks like. The leader who has never honestly assessed her own strength, weakness, blind spots, and breaking points will, in crisis moments, either collapse under others' burdens or unconsciously transfer her own unprocessed weight onto those she is trying to serve (Banks & Ledbetter, 2004).

Notice also what Paul says about self-deception: "If anyone thinks they are something when they are not, they deceive themselves." The Greek word for "test" in this passage (dokimazō) is the same word used for assaying metal — the process of heating an ore to determine its true composition. Honest self-assessment, in Paul's theology, is not ego-driven introspection. It is the act of submitting one's life to the refining heat of truthful examination so that what is genuine can be distinguished from what is merely imagined.

The second passage, from Romans 12, introduces the phrase that functions as DVULI's north star for this entire session: think of yourself with sober judgment. Banks and Ledbetter (2004) argue persuasively that this phrase is neither a call to false modesty nor to self-promotion. Paul is calling for something far more demanding — what we might translate as clear-eyed, faith-grounded sobriety about who you actually are. Not more highly than you ought. Not less honestly than you should. But with the uncommon clarity that comes from seeing yourself through the lens of God's grace rather than through the distorting mirrors of ego protection or shame.

And crucially, Romans 12 situates this self-knowledge within the body of Christ. You are not called to assess yourself in isolation, comparing yourself to your own private benchmark. You are called to understand yourself as a member of a body — uniquely gifted, uniquely called, uniquely placed. The self-knowledge that Romans 12 invites is inherently communal: it is self-knowledge in service of the whole body, and it is best developed in community with that body. This is why the DVULI city group — the community of peers you are beginning this journey with — is not merely a learning convenience. It is a theological necessity. The journey cannot be traveled alone.

Think About It

Before reading on, consider: when was the last time someone who knew you well offered you an honest assessment of your strengths or blind spots — and you genuinely received it? What made that possible, or what made it difficult? How might a community intentionally committed to "sober judgment" look different from ordinary organizational feedback?

Pillar Two: The Three-Fold Purpose Framework

The DVULI Personal Assessment process begins with purpose — not because purpose questions are easy, but because they are foundational. Before you can understand the dimensions of who you are, you need a framework for understanding what you are for. The program draws on Richard Bolles' landmark work, What Color Is Your Parachute? (Bolles, 2021), which introduces a three-part framework for personal mission that has profoundly shaped vocational discernment curricula for decades.

Dik and Duffy (2012) observe that a sense of calling — of having a transcendent purpose that guides one's work — is not reserved for clergy or extraordinary individuals. It is available to any person through a process of intentional self-reflection, feedback, and community discernment. The three-fold framework from Bolles gives that process a structural home. Let's explore each dimension.

Purpose Part One: To Know God, and Enjoy Him Forever

The first purpose — to know God and enjoy Him forever — is not a pious preface to the "real" parts of the framework. It is, in the deepest sense, the entire point. Every other dimension of divine design flows from the quality of a leader's relationship with the living God. A youth ministry that is built on techniques, passion, and community need — but not on an ongoing, deepening encounter with the God who called the leader to that work — will eventually run dry. The reservoir is the relationship. The DVULI program's commitment to mentoring, journaling, reflective practice, and prayerful community is grounded precisely in this conviction: that sustainable ministry leadership requires a leader who is actively, honestly, and joyfully in relationship with God (Bolles, 2021).

As the DVULI workbook frames it: our purpose doesn't change, but our understanding and growth in it may. As our roles or settings change, the way we express this purpose may stay the same, shift, or expand. The question "Where are you in relation to this purpose?" is not a test to pass. It is a diagnostic — a gentle pressure point that reveals where growth is needed and where gratitude is warranted.

Purpose Part Two: To Make the World a Better Place

The second purpose — to do what you can, moment by moment, day by day, step by step, to make this world a better place, following the leading and guidance of God's Spirit — is your vocational purpose. It is prosocial at its core. Dik and Duffy (2009) identify this prosocial orientation as one of three essential dimensions of calling: a transcendent summons, meaningful work, and the drive to contribute to something beyond oneself. The leader who has genuinely internalized this second purpose cannot be primarily motivated by personal advancement, institutional preservation, or the applause of stakeholders. She is compelled, at the deepest level, by love for the community she serves.

Note the temporal humility built into Bolles' language: moment by moment, day by day, step by step. Purpose Part Two is not a destination to arrive at — it is a process to inhabit. Your purpose becomes clearer over time as you implement it. It deepens as you see it through different perspectives. This is one reason the DVULI program does not expect participants to arrive at the First Local Workshop with a polished, final purpose statement. You are expected to arrive honestly — to bring where you actually are, not where you think you should be.

Purpose Part Three: To Use Your Greatest Gift

The third purpose is the most individual: to exercise the talent you particularly came to Earth to use — your greatest gift, which you most delight to use, in the place God has designed for you, and for the purposes God most needs done in the world. Bolles calls this your "Flower" — the intersection of your peak skills, your deepest values, the settings that energize you, and the problems you feel most compelled to solve (Bolles, 2021).

Bill George (2003) calls this a leader's True North — the internal compass that is built through experience, reflection, and honest self-assessment, and that orients every major leadership decision. The leader who has done the work of identifying her greatest gift is not easily seduced by ministry opportunities that look important but are actually a poor fit. She can say no to good things because she is clear about what she is for. She can absorb criticism without being shattered and receive affirmation without being inflated, because her identity is anchored not in performance or reputation, but in the clear-eyed knowledge of what God has uniquely equipped her to do.

Pillar Three: The DVULI Personal Assessment Model

With the three-fold purpose framework providing the "why" of your leadership, the DVULI Personal Assessment Model provides the "who." It is the program's primary diagnostic framework for understanding the leader as a whole person — not merely as a skill set or a role, but as an integrated human being whose effectiveness in ministry is inseparable from her interior life, her values, and her willingness to grow (Avolio, 2005).

The model is organized around three foundational dimensions and three resulting qualities that emerge when those dimensions are integrated. This is not a static inventory of personality traits. It is a dynamic map of the capacities required to lead, learn, and serve sustainably over time.

The Three Core Dimensions

The first core dimension is Calling — defined in the DVULI framework as "faithfully doing the unique work that God has called and equipped me to do as my contribution to building His Kingdom." Calling, in this sense, is more than career satisfaction or vocational interest. Dik and Duffy (2009) describe genuine calling as encompassing a transcendent summons (a sense of being called from beyond oneself), purposeful work (meaning-making activity), and prosocial motivation (orientation toward the good of others). The DVULI definition aligns precisely with all three of these dimensions. Calling is not what you do when you have time. It is the central organizing principle of your leadership life — the work you were uniquely made for, and that the Kingdom most needs you to do.

The second core dimension is Character — defined as "consistently living out the values I espouse, applying them to my every action and decision." Character, in this framework, is not simply about avoiding moral failure. It is about the alignment — or misalignment — between what a leader professes and what she actually practices. Peterson and Seligman (2004), in their landmark classification of character strengths and virtues, argue that character is both stable and developable: virtues can be identified, assessed, and cultivated through intentional practice. The VIA Classification provides exactly the kind of measurable vocabulary for character that the DVULI model requires. When DVULI asks whether you are "consistently living out the values you espouse," it is pressing on the question of integration: are the values displayed on your ministry's website the same values driving your decisions at 11 p.m. when no one is watching?

The third core dimension is Commitment to Change — defined as "willingly adjusting my thoughts, plans, and actions to align them more fully with God's will for my life and ministry." This dimension is, in many respects, the hardest of the three. Calling can be energizing. Character can feel like natural virtue. But Commitment to Change requires the leader to hold every assumption, every strategy, every familiar way of doing things in an open hand — willing to release what is comfortable in favor of what is faithful. George (2003) describes this quality as the opposite of what he calls "imposter syndrome" — the tendency to project a confident, settled self-image while privately refusing to examine the gaps between aspiration and reality. The leader with genuine Commitment to Change welcomes the discomfort of honest feedback precisely because she values alignment with God's will over the comfort of self-protection.

The Three Resulting Qualities

When these three dimensions are integrated, the DVULI model identifies three qualities that mark the leader who is genuinely ready to learn and grow. These are not additional competencies to acquire — they are the natural fruit of Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change working together.

Authenticity (Calling + Character) is defined as "integrating both doing what God calls me to do and being who God has designed me to be." George (2003) argues that authentic leadership begins precisely here — in the convergence of purpose and integrity. The authentic leader is not performing a role. She is operating from the deepest truth of who she is. When calling and character are integrated, the leader's outward ministry and inward life reinforce rather than contradict each other. There is no performance gap — no exhausting distance between the person people see and the person who lies awake at night.

Responsiveness (Calling + Commitment to Change) is defined as "having the attitude of Christ — being willing to submit my will to God's will, as modeled by Jesus in his responsiveness to people, circumstances, and most importantly, His Father's will." The responsive leader is not reactive in the sense of being blown about by every wind of circumstance. She is responsive in the deeper sense of remaining open — to the Spirit's leading, to the community's need, to the invitation to revise her plans in light of God's larger purposes. This is the quality that keeps vocational leaders from becoming prisoners of their own previous commitments.

Teachability (Character + Commitment to Change) is defined as "wanting to be transformed through the renewing of my mind — being a learner rather than a knower." This is the quality that makes the entire DVULI experience possible. A leader who enters the program already certain of what she knows — about urban ministry, about her community, about herself — will extract relatively little from the learning opportunities ahead. A leader who enters as a genuine learner, willing to have her assumptions examined and her certainties tested, will be transformed. Eurich (2017) identifies teachability — the willingness to seek and receive truthful feedback from multiple sources — as one of the most consistent predictors of high self-awareness. It is no coincidence that DVULI places it at the very foundation of the learning journey.

Three Paths to Self-Knowledge: How Discovery Actually Happens

The DVULI Pre-Assignment identifies three distinct methods through which leaders can gain genuine self-knowledge. What is crucial to understand about these three methods is that none of them is complete by itself. Each illuminates a different dimension of who you are, and each has characteristic blind spots that the others help correct. The leader who relies on only one method is like a person who insists on navigating by only one star — likely to drift off course without ever realizing it.

Method One: Reflecting on Personal Experience

The DVULI framework describes reflection on personal experience as "the most useful source, provided you have a clear framework to guide your reflection." The qualification is important. Unguided introspection — the kind of circular self-examination that goes over the same ground again and again without deepening understanding — is not what the program intends. Douglas Rumford (cited in the DVULI Pre-Assignment materials) describes the difference between a diary and a genuine learning journal: the learning journal is "a place where you reflect intelligently on what and how you are learning, including how you are applying these lessons and what outcomes you are achieving."

Eurich (2017) offers an important caution about introspection that DVULI takes seriously: research shows that introspection can actually decrease accuracy rather than increase it, if the introspection is focused on the wrong questions. The question "Why do I do this?" often leads to confabulation — the construction of plausible-sounding explanations that feel true but are not. The more productive question is "What?" — "What patterns do I notice in my behavior? What circumstances seem to activate my best and worst responses? What have I learned from the experiences of the past year?" The Purpose in Life exercises in the Pre-Assignment are designed to channel personal reflection toward exactly these productive questions.

Method Two: Feedback from Others

The second method — receiving honest feedback from people who know you — addresses the fundamental limitation of all purely internal self-assessment: you cannot see yourself the way others see you. Eurich (2017) distinguishes between internal self-awareness (how clearly you see your own values, passions, and patterns) and external self-awareness (how accurately you understand how others experience you). High performers in both dimensions — what Eurich calls "unicorns" — are consistently better leaders, decision-makers, and relationship-builders. But the two forms of self-awareness are not automatically correlated: you can have a great deal of self-knowledge in one dimension while being almost completely blind in the other.

This is why the DVULI city group — the community of peers you are journeying with — is structured to provide ongoing, honest, constructive feedback throughout the program. The Core Values Mentor relationship is designed for the same purpose. Paul's instruction to "carry each other's burdens" is not only about crisis support; it is about the everyday work of seeing each other clearly and speaking what we see with love. The program builds in multiple structured feedback opportunities precisely because the leaders who most need an honest mirror are often the ones least likely to seek one voluntarily.

Method Three: Formal Evaluation

The third method — formal assessment tools — provides the kind of standardized, validated, comparative data that personal reflection and peer feedback cannot generate. The DVULI Pre-Assignment introduces two formal tools: the Biblical DISC Assessment, which helps identify patterns in behavioral style and communication preference, and the Leadership Strategy Assessment (LSA), which maps preferred leadership strategies across four key approaches: Instructing, Developing, Mentoring, and Commissioning.

The purpose of these tools is not to put participants in boxes or reduce complex human beings to four letters or a graph pattern. The DVULI workbook is explicit: "there is no one 'right' style." Rather, formal evaluation provides a shared vocabulary for discussing behavioral patterns — including both strengths and potential overuse — in ways that can catalyze genuine insight and productive community conversation. Peterson and Seligman's (2004) work on character strengths similarly demonstrates that naming and measuring one's strengths — rather than focusing exclusively on deficits — produces significantly greater growth and resilience over time.

📌 DVULI's Position: All Three Are Necessary — None Is Complete Alone

The Pre-Assignment is clear: "All of these methods provide windows into you; none is complete by itself. Consider them all as you grow in your own self-awareness." Your personal ranking reveals something important about which source of feedback you naturally seek — and which you may tend to avoid.

Which of the three methods have you used least in the past year?

The Leadership Tree: Rooting Your Identity

The DVULI Leadership Tree provides a powerful visual metaphor for integrating everything you are discovering in this session. The tree's roots represent your purpose — the invisible but essential foundation that determines whether the whole tree will be able to sustain weight, weather storms, and bear fruit over time. The trunk represents the people who support you — the relationships without which no leader can thrive in the long term. And the leaves and branches represent what is visible to others — your behavioral style, your leadership strategies, your observable patterns of action.

A healthy tree can live for a very long time, but one with damaged or under-developed parts soon dies. Likewise, a leader is only effective when she uses all parts of her divine design together to create a strong root system, a sturdy trunk, and a full set of branches. This is why the Personal Assessment process does not stop at purpose — it moves through behavioral style (DISC), leadership strategy (LSA), and community formation (the city group) before the First Local Workshop concludes. You are not simply completing exercises. You are building the architecture of a leadership life that can last.

Avolio (2005) argues that sustainable leader development must begin with identity — with a leader's deeply held sense of who she is, what she values, and how she is experienced by others. His concept of developmental readiness — the degree to which a leader is motivated to learn and genuinely capable of self-examination — maps directly onto what DVULI calls Teachability. The leaders who gain the most from programs like DVULI are not the most polished or experienced. They are the ones who arrive the most genuinely open — ready to be surprised, challenged, and changed by what they find in the mirror of honest self-knowledge.

Think About It

As you prepare to complete your Leadership Tree poster, ask yourself: Which part of your tree feels strongest right now — roots (purpose), trunk (support relationships), or branches (visible behavioral style and strategy)? Which part is most in need of development? What would it mean for your ministry if you invested intentional energy there this year?

Honest Self-Knowledge as an Act of Worship

We began this session with a question that stopped a seasoned ministry leader cold: Who are you? It is worth returning to that question now, not to answer it definitively — you will be working on that answer for the rest of this program, and in some sense for the rest of your life — but to understand what is at stake in taking it seriously.

Paul's instruction in Romans 12 is deliberately embedded in a passage about worship. "Offer your bodies as a living sacrifice," he writes — and then, in the next breath, he calls for the renewal of the mind and sober self-judgment. This is not a coincidence. In Paul's theology, honest self-assessment is not a therapeutic exercise or a professional development activity. It is a form of worship. To see yourself clearly — your strengths, your gifts, your calling, your character gaps, your behavioral patterns, your blind spots — and to offer that honest seeing back to God as the starting point for a life of obedient service: this is what the renewed mind looks like in practice.

The leader who refuses to do this work — who hides behind busyness, institutional role, or the flattery of those who depend on her — is not merely missing a development opportunity. She is, in Paul's language, "deceiving herself." She is offering God a performance rather than a person. The reward for stepping out in faith on a learning journey, as the DVULI Pre-Assignment puts it, is that you will know who you are at your best — the unique person God has created you to be, and the unique work for which He has called and equipped you. That reward is worth the discomfort of the question. In fact, no other starting point will do.

Key Takeaways

  • Honest self-knowledge is not narcissism — it is a form of worship and a prerequisite for genuine burden-bearing community (Romans 12:1–5; Galatians 6:2–5).
  • Paul's call to "sober judgment" means seeing yourself neither more highly than you ought nor less honestly than you should — through the clear lens of faith-grounded identity.
  • The Three-Fold Purpose Framework (Bolles, 2021) provides the foundation for understanding what you are for: to know God, to make the world better, and to use your greatest gift in the place God has designed for you.
  • The DVULI Personal Assessment Model organizes leadership identity around three core dimensions — Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change — which together produce Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability.
  • Authentic leadership (George, 2003) begins with self-awareness: the consistent alignment between espoused values and enacted behavior, anchored in a clear sense of True North.
  • Self-knowledge is built through three complementary methods — personal reflection, feedback from others, and formal evaluation — none of which is complete without the other two (Eurich, 2017).
  • The DISC and LSA assessments are not boxes to fit into; they are shared vocabulary tools for understanding behavioral patterns and engaging in productive community conversation.
  • Developmental readiness — the genuine willingness to learn, grow, and be changed — is the most important quality you bring to the DVULI journey (Avolio, 2005).

Looking Ahead

Session 2 builds on the foundation laid here by bringing your assessment data into community conversation. Having completed your DISC and LSA reports, you will explore what your behavioral style and leadership strategy patterns mean for how you show up in ministry — and, crucially, how your patterns interact with those of the colleagues sitting across from you in the city group. The question will shift from "Who am I?" to "Who are we together — and what can we build because of our differences?"

Dik, B. J., & Duffy, R. D. (2012). Make your job a calling: How the psychology of vocation can change your life at work. Templeton Press. https://www.templeton.org

Eurich, T. (2017). Insight: The surprising truth about how others see us, how we see ourselves, and why the answers matter more than we think. Crown Business. https://www.penguinrandomhouse.com

George, B. (2003). Authentic leadership: Rediscovering the secrets to creating lasting value. Jossey-Bass. https://www.hbs.edu

Peterson, C., & Seligman, M. E. P. (2004). Character strengths and virtues: A handbook and classification. Oxford University Press. https://global.oup.com

Rumford, D. J. (1981). The value of a personal journal. Leadership Journal. [Reprinted in DVULI Local Workshop 1 Pre-Assignment, 2024].

blocks.title: Key Concepts
concepts: Three-fold personal purpose framework: knowing God, making the world better, using your greatest gift
Personal Assessment Model: Calling + Character + Commitment to Change → Authenticity, Responsiveness, Teachability
Biblical basis for honest self-assessment: sober judgment, renewing the mind, burden-bearing community
Three methods of self-discovery: reflecting on personal experience, feedback from others, formal evaluation

items.question: According to the session's three-fold personal purpose framework, what are the three elements of a person's purpose?
items.correctAnswer: To know God, to make the world a better place, and to use your greatest gift in the place God designed for you
items.correctFeedback: The three-fold framework — vertical relationship with God, horizontal impact on the world, and stewardship of one's unique design — holds together as a unit; answers that name only one or two dimensions describe a piece of calling, not its full scope. Return to the Parachute framework section and test whether each element is present before concluding the picture is complete.

distractors.text: To develop character, to serve the church, and to pursue professional excellence
distractors.feedback: Students might select this because character and service are prominent themes in the session. However, the three-fold purpose framework specifically names knowing God, improving the world, and using one's greatest gift — not professional excellence or church service as separate categories.

distractors.text: To practice sober judgment, to bear one another's burdens, and to renew the mind
distractors.feedback: These are biblical principles discussed in the session from Romans and Galatians, but they are not the components of the three-fold purpose framework. They serve as the biblical foundation for self-assessment rather than the purpose framework itself.

distractors.text: To discover your calling, to develop authenticity through vulnerability, and to commit to lifelong learning
distractors.feedback: This answer blends language from the Personal Assessment Model (calling, authenticity) with general leadership concepts. While these ideas appear in the session, they do not constitute the three-fold purpose framework from What Color Is Your Parachute?

items.question: What does Paul mean by instructing believers to think of themselves with 'sober judgment' as used in this session?
items.correctAnswer: To assess yourself with clear-eyed honesty — neither inflating your strengths nor minimizing your weaknesses
items.correctFeedback: Sober judgment, drawn from Romans 12, is the session's north star for self-assessment. It calls for a balanced, faith-grounded view of oneself that avoids both arrogance and false humility, enabling genuine self-knowledge as an act of worship.

distractors.text: To adopt a posture of humility by focusing primarily on your weaknesses and areas for growth
distractors.feedback: Students may choose this because humility is often associated with focusing on flaws. However, sober judgment explicitly rejects thinking too lowly of yourself just as it rejects thinking too highly — it demands honest balance, not lopsided self-criticism.

distractors.text: To evaluate yourself strictly by comparing your abilities to those of other leaders around you, a practice often called social norming in leadership development literature
distractors.feedback: Comparative evaluation might seem like a practical way to be 'sober,' but the passage grounds identity in faith, not in comparison to others. Sober judgment is about clear self-perception rooted in God's design, not competitive ranking.

distractors.text: To withhold any self-assessment until you have received confirmation from your community
distractors.feedback: While community feedback is one of the three methods of self-discovery discussed in the session, sober judgment is a personal responsibility Paul places on each believer. It is not about deferring all self-knowledge to others.

items.question: In the DVULI Personal Assessment Model, what are the three foundational dimensions?
items.correctAnswer: Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change
items.correctFeedback: These three dimensions form the core of the Personal Assessment Model. Calling addresses faithfully doing God's unique work, Character addresses consistently living out one's values, and Commitment to Change addresses willingness to adjust thoughts and actions to align with God's will.

distractors.text: Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability
distractors.feedback: These three terms are closely related to the model but represent the outcomes or marks that result from the three foundational dimensions, not the dimensions themselves. Understanding this input-output relationship is key to grasping the model.

distractors.text: Calling, Competence, and Community
distractors.feedback: Calling is indeed one of the three dimensions, which makes this answer tempting. However, the model uses Character and Commitment to Change — not Competence and Community — as the other two foundational pillars.

distractors.text: Self-awareness, Spiritual discipline, and Service orientation
distractors.feedback: While these sound like reasonable leadership categories and relate to broader themes in the session, they are not the specific dimensions named in the DVULI Personal Assessment Model.

items.question: According to the session, why is honest self-assessment considered a form of worship rather than narcissism?
items.correctAnswer: Because Romans 12 frames renewing the mind and offering oneself to God as a spiritual act of worship that requires knowing who you truly are
items.correctFeedback: The session draws on Romans 12:1–5, where Paul connects the renewed mind and self-offering to worship. Honest self-assessment becomes worship when it serves the purpose of understanding God's design so you can offer yourself more fully. This biblical framing removes the stigma of self-focus.

distractors.text: Because focusing on yourself is always spiritually beneficial as long as you do it in a church setting
distractors.feedback: The session does not argue that any self-focus is automatically spiritual or that a church setting makes it so. The key distinction is the purpose and biblical grounding of the assessment — it is worship because it serves God's purposes, not simply because of where it occurs.

distractors.text: Because leaders who serve others earn the right to focus on themselves periodically
distractors.feedback: While the session acknowledges that leaders who pour into others often neglect self-understanding, the biblical warrant is not about earning a right. It is rooted in Paul's theology of worship through the renewed mind, not in a transactional model of service and self-care.

distractors.text: Because the book of Galatians explicitly commands every believer to complete a formal self-assessment annually
distractors.feedback: Galatians 6:2–5 is indeed one of the session's key passages, but it does not prescribe an annual formal self-assessment. The passage discusses burden-bearing and testing one's own work — the session applies these principles to self-knowledge, not as a literal command for annual evaluation.

items.question: What are the three methods of self-discovery presented in this session?
items.correctAnswer: Reflecting on personal experience, receiving feedback from others, and completing formal evaluations
items.correctFeedback: The session identifies these three complementary methods as pathways to self-knowledge. Personal reflection provides internal insight, feedback from others reveals blind spots, and formal evaluations offer structured, objective data — together they create a comprehensive picture.

distractors.text: Prayer and fasting, Scripture memorization, and accountability partnerships
distractors.feedback: These are legitimate spiritual disciplines, but they are not the three methods of self-discovery outlined in this session. The session's methods are more broadly applicable to understanding personal strengths, blind spots, and behavioral patterns.

distractors.text: Reading leadership books, attending conferences, and journaling daily
distractors.feedback: While these activities could support personal development, the session specifically names reflecting on personal experience, feedback from others, and formal evaluation as its three methods — a more structured and interpersonally grounded approach.

distractors.text: Identifying spiritual gifts, taking personality tests, and studying biblical characters
distractors.feedback: Some of these overlap with elements of the session (formal evaluations could include personality tests), but this list does not match the three methods as presented. The session frames the methods more broadly as personal reflection, community feedback, and formal tools.

items.question: A youth ministry leader receives difficult feedback from a colleague suggesting she tends to dominate team meetings. She initially feels defensive but then prays, reflects honestly, and decides to practice listening more intentionally. Which outcome of the Personal Assessment Model does her response best illustrate?
items.correctAnswer: Teachability, which flows from Commitment to Change
items.correctFeedback: Teachability is the mark of a leader who willingly adjusts thoughts, plans, and actions to align more fully with God's will. By moving through defensiveness to honest reflection and behavioral change, this leader demonstrates the Commitment to Change dimension producing its corresponding outcome.

distractors.text: Authenticity, which flows from Calling
distractors.feedback: Authenticity relates to faithfully doing the unique work God has called and equipped you to do. While the leader is being genuine, the scenario specifically highlights her willingness to adjust behavior based on feedback — which points to Teachability, not Authenticity.

distractors.text: Responsiveness, which flows from Commitment to Change
distractors.feedback: Responsiveness is indeed one of the three outcomes, but it flows from Character (consistently living out espoused values), not from Commitment to Change. The scenario emphasizes willingness to adjust behavior, which aligns with Teachability as the outcome of Commitment to Change.

distractors.text: Sober judgment, which flows from Character
distractors.feedback: Sober judgment is a biblical concept from Romans 12 that undergirds the entire self-assessment process, but it is not one of the three outcomes of the Personal Assessment Model. The model's outcomes are Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability.

items.question: How does Galatians 6:2–5 contribute to the session's argument for self-knowledge?
items.correctAnswer: It establishes that each person should test their own work and carry their own load, while also bearing one another's burdens in community
items.correctFeedback: Galatians 6:2–5 provides a dual mandate: communal burden-bearing alongside individual responsibility to 'test your own actions.' This passage grounds self-assessment in community care — knowing yourself well is necessary both for personal responsibility and for contributing authentically to others.

distractors.text: It provides a list of spiritual gifts that each believer should use to identify their unique calling
distractors.feedback: Spiritual gifts lists appear elsewhere in Scripture (such as Romans 12 and 1 Corinthians 12), but Galatians 6:2–5 does not contain a gifts list. Its contribution to the session is about personal responsibility, self-testing, and communal burden-bearing.

distractors.text: It warns believers against any form of self-examination because it leads to pride and comparison
distractors.feedback: This represents the opposite of the passage's actual teaching. Galatians 6 encourages self-testing ('each one should test their own actions') and cautions against comparison — it supports self-examination rather than prohibiting it.

distractors.text: It commands leaders to submit their personal assessments to church elders for validation before acting on them
distractors.feedback: While community is part of the session's framework, Galatians 6:2–5 does not prescribe elder-validated assessments. The passage balances communal care (bearing burdens) with personal accountability (carrying your own load and testing your own work).

items.question: In the Personal Assessment Model, Character is defined as consistently living out the values you espouse. Which of the following outcomes does Character produce?
items.correctFeedback: In the model, the three dimensions each produce a corresponding outcome: Calling produces Authenticity, Character produces Responsiveness, and Commitment to Change produces Teachability. Responsiveness emerges when a leader's lived behavior consistently aligns with their stated values, enabling them to respond with integrity.

distractors.text: Authenticity
distractors.feedback: Authenticity is indeed one of the three outcomes of the model, but it corresponds to Calling — faithfully doing the unique work God has called you to do — not to Character. Mixing up which dimension produces which outcome is a common error.

distractors.text: Teachability
distractors.feedback: Teachability flows from Commitment to Change, not from Character. While a person of strong character may also be teachable, the model specifically links Teachability to the willingness to adjust thoughts, plans, and actions.

distractors.text: Sober judgment
distractors.feedback: Sober judgment is a foundational biblical concept from Romans 12 that informs the entire self-assessment process, but it is not one of the three outcomes produced by the Personal Assessment Model's dimensions.

items.question: Why does the session argue that many leaders who serve in youth ministry have difficulty with self-assessment?
items.correctAnswer: Because they have spent years pouring into others and have never taken time to understand their own strengths, blind spots, and behavioral patterns
items.correctFeedback: The session identifies a common pattern among dedicated ministry leaders: their outward focus on serving others has come at the expense of self-understanding. This is precisely why the program creates intentional space for self-discovery — these leaders have the motivation to serve but often lack self-knowledge.

distractors.text: Because they lack formal theological education and therefore cannot interpret the relevant Scripture passages
distractors.feedback: The session does not attribute the difficulty to lack of theological education. The issue is not intellectual or educational but practical — these leaders have prioritized serving others over examining themselves, regardless of their educational background.

distractors.text: Because their organizations actively discourage self-reflection as a distraction from the mission, viewing introspection as a threat to team cohesion
distractors.feedback: While some organizational cultures may not prioritize reflection, the session does not cite organizational opposition as the reason. The challenge is personal — leaders simply have not made space for self-understanding amid their outward-focused ministry work.

distractors.text: Because self-assessment tools are generally unreliable and unavailable in ministry contexts
distractors.feedback: The session actually promotes formal evaluation tools as one of three valid methods of self-discovery. The difficulty is not about tool availability but about leaders' tendency to neglect self-examination while focusing on the needs of others.

items.question: A participant completes the session and articulates that her purpose is to deepen her relationship with Christ, advocate for at-risk teens in her city, and leverage her gift of strategic communication in youth outreach. Which framework is she applying?
items.correctAnswer: The three-fold personal purpose framework from What Color Is Your Parachute?
items.correctFeedback: Her three statements map precisely onto the framework's components: knowing God and enjoying Him forever (deepening her relationship with Christ), making the world a better place (advocating for at-risk teens), and using her greatest gift in the place God designed (leveraging strategic communication in youth outreach).

distractors.text: The DVULI Personal Assessment Model of Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change
distractors.feedback: The Personal Assessment Model focuses on three dimensions of leadership readiness — faithfully fulfilling God's call, living consistently with your values, and willingness to adjust. While her statement touches on calling, it does not address character or commitment to change, and its structure matches the purpose framework instead.

distractors.text: Paul's sober judgment framework from Romans 12:1–5
distractors.feedback: Sober judgment is a guiding principle for honest self-assessment, not a structured framework that produces a three-part personal purpose statement. Her articulation of knowing God, improving the world, and using her gift follows the three-fold purpose framework, not the Romans 12 teaching specifically.

distractors.text: The Galatians 6 model of burden-bearing and self-testing
distractors.feedback: Galatians 6:2–5 provides the biblical basis for balancing communal care with personal responsibility, but it is not a framework for articulating personal purpose. Her three-part purpose statement clearly follows the structure of the three-fold framework from What Color Is Your Parachute?

questions.stem: According to the three-fold personal purpose framework drawn from What Color Is Your Parachute?, which of the following is NOT one of the three life purposes?
options: To build a successful career in ministry
To know God and enjoy Him forever
To make the world a better place
To use your greatest gift in the place God has designed for you

feedback.correct: The three-fold purpose framework identifies knowing God, making the world better, and using your greatest gift — not building a career. Career success may follow, but it is not named as one of the three foundational purposes.
feedback.incorrect: Review the three-fold purpose framework carefully. Each purpose is oriented toward God's design and calling rather than professional achievement. The three are: know God and enjoy Him forever, make the world a better place, and use your greatest gift where God has placed you.

variants.stem: Which of the following IS one of the three life purposes in the three-fold purpose framework from What Color Is Your Parachute?
options: To use your greatest gift in the place God has designed for you
To develop your leadership platform and influence
To identify your spiritual gifts through formal testing
To build authentic community with other ministry leaders

feedback.correct: Using your greatest gift in the place God has designed for you is one of the three purposes alongside knowing God and making the world better. Notice how this purpose connects gifting to divine placement.
feedback.incorrect: While community, leadership, and spiritual gifts are all discussed in the session, only three specific purposes make up the framework. They are: know God and enjoy Him forever, make the world a better place, and use your greatest gift where God has placed you.

questions.stem: Paul's instruction to 'think of yourself with sober judgment' — the session's north star for self-assessment — is drawn from which Scripture passage?
options: Romans 12:1–5
Galatians 6:2–5
Ephesians 4:1–6
Philippians 2:1–4

feedback.correct: Romans 12:1–5 contains Paul's instruction about sober judgment and renewing the mind. The session pairs this with Galatians 6:2–5 on burden-bearing community, but the 'sober judgment' language specifically comes from Romans 12.
feedback.incorrect: Two passages form the session's biblical foundation: Romans 12:1–5 and Galatians 6:2–5. The sober judgment instruction comes from Romans 12, while Galatians 6 addresses burden-bearing community and each person carrying their own load.

variants.stem: The session's concept of burden-bearing community as a context for self-knowledge is primarily drawn from which biblical passage?
options: Galatians 6:2–5
Romans 12:1–5
Colossians 3:12–17
1 Corinthians 12:4–11

feedback.correct: Galatians 6:2–5 provides the biblical basis for burden-bearing community. This passage teaches both mutual burden-bearing and individual responsibility — two sides of the communal self-knowledge coin.
feedback.incorrect: The session builds on two key passages. Galatians 6:2–5 addresses community, mutual burden-bearing, and individual responsibility. Romans 12:1–5 addresses sober judgment and renewing the mind. Don't confuse which theme comes from which passage.

questions.stem: The session identifies three methods for self-discovery. Which of the following correctly lists all three?
options: Reflecting on personal experience, feedback from others, and formal evaluation
Prayer and fasting, Scripture meditation, and pastoral counseling
Personality tests, spiritual gifts inventories, and mentor assessments
Journaling, group discussion, and academic study

feedback.correct: The three methods are reflecting on personal experience, receiving feedback from others, and completing formal evaluations. Together they provide a multi-angle view of yourself that no single method could offer alone.
feedback.incorrect: While prayer, journaling, and spiritual assessments are valuable practices, the session specifically names three methods: personal reflection, feedback from others, and formal evaluation. These represent internal, relational, and structured approaches to self-knowledge.

variants.stem: A DVULI participant wants to follow the session's recommended approaches to self-discovery. Which combination correctly represents the three methods taught in the session?
options: Personal experience reflection, community feedback, and formal assessment tools
Bible study, intercessory prayer, and fasting
Peer comparison, competitive benchmarking, and self-grading
Meditation, solitude retreats, and counseling sessions

feedback.correct: The session teaches three methods: reflecting on your own experience, hearing from others who know you, and using formal evaluation instruments. This trio ensures you aren't relying on just one lens to see yourself.
feedback.incorrect: The session names a specific trio of methods: personal reflection, feedback from others, and formal evaluations. These correspond to internal, relational, and structured sources of insight — each compensating for the others' limitations.

questions.stem: A youth pastor scores high on Calling and Character in the DVULI Personal Assessment Model but consistently resists suggestions for how she could improve her teaching style. According to the model, what is she most likely lacking?
options: Commitment to Change
Calling
Character
Sober judgment
justifications: Because Commitment to Change is defined as willingly adjusting thoughts, plans, and actions to align more fully with God's will, and her resistance to improvement suggestions directly conflicts with this dimension
Because anyone who resists feedback automatically lacks Calling since Calling requires openness to all input
Because Character is defined as accepting every piece of criticism you receive without question
Because sober judgment is a fourth dimension of the Personal Assessment Model that she has not yet developed

feedback.correct: Commitment to Change is specifically defined as willingly adjusting your thoughts, plans, and actions to align with God's will. A leader can be genuinely called and live with integrity yet still resist the adjustments needed for growth. This is why all three dimensions are necessary.
feedback.incorrect: Look carefully at the definitions of each dimension. Calling is about faithfully doing what God equipped you to do; Character is about consistently living your values. Resistance to improvement aligns most directly with a deficit in Commitment to Change — the willingness to adjust.
feedback.wrongReason: Students sometimes correctly identify Commitment to Change but then justify it by confusing it with Character (values consistency) or by treating sober judgment as a separate model dimension rather than a biblical concept that undergirds the entire framework.

variants.stem: A ministry leader is passionate about his work and readily accepts coaching on new strategies, but colleagues notice that his private behavior often contradicts the values he preaches publicly. According to the DVULI Personal Assessment Model, which dimension is he most likely lacking?
options: Character
Calling
Commitment to Change
Authenticity
justifications: Because Character is defined as consistently living out the values you espouse, and the gap between his public teaching and private behavior directly violates this dimension
Because Calling requires that a leader's public and private lives match perfectly at all times
Because Commitment to Change means ensuring your behavior never contradicts your words
Because Authenticity is a foundational dimension of the model that he has failed to develop

feedback.correct: Character is defined as consistently living out the values you espouse. A gap between public teaching and private conduct is the clearest indicator of a Character deficit. Note that Authenticity is an output quality, not an input dimension of the model.
feedback.incorrect: Distinguish carefully between the three input dimensions. The inconsistency between public values and private behavior maps directly to Character — 'consistently living out the values I espouse.' Calling is about doing your unique work; Commitment to Change is about willingness to adjust.
feedback.wrongReason: Students sometimes choose the right answer but then confuse Character's definition with Calling or Commitment to Change. Remember: Character is specifically about the consistency between espoused values and actual behavior — nothing more, nothing less.

questions.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
questions.assertion: The DVULI session teaches that honest self-assessment is a form of worship and community care rather than an act of narcissism.
questions.reason: Paul instructs believers in Romans 12 to think of themselves with sober judgment, grounding self-evaluation in faith-based identity rather than ego, and presenting one's body as a living sacrifice.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, and the Romans 12 passage is precisely the biblical warrant the session uses to reframe self-assessment as worship. Paul connects offering your body as a living sacrifice (worship) with renewing the mind and thinking with sober judgment — making self-knowledge an act of devotion, not self-absorption.
feedback.incorrect: Consider each statement independently first. The session explicitly calls self-assessment a form of worship and community care. Then ask whether Romans 12's teaching about sober judgment and living sacrifice is the actual reason the session makes this claim — it is the direct biblical foundation cited.

variants.assertion: The DVULI Personal Assessment Model requires all three dimensions — Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change — to produce Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability.
variants.reason: Calling alone is sufficient to produce Authenticity because a leader who is faithful to their calling will naturally develop the other qualities.

feedback.correct: The assertion is true — the model explicitly states that the three dimensions 'taken together' produce the three output qualities. The reason is false because Calling alone is not sufficient; the model insists on the integration of all three dimensions. No single dimension generates the full set of qualities.
feedback.incorrect: Read the reason carefully. Does the session teach that Calling alone is enough? The Personal Assessment Model emphasizes that the three dimensions must work 'taken together.' A leader with strong Calling but weak Character or no Commitment to Change would not exhibit all three qualities.

questions.stem: According to the session, 'sober judgment' in self-assessment means thinking of yourself:
options: Not more highly than you ought, and not less honestly than you should
As less important than everyone else to maintain humility
Only in terms of your spiritual gifts and calling
Through the lens of your greatest accomplishments and successes

feedback.correct: Sober judgment is the session's north star: a balanced, clear-eyed assessment that avoids both inflated self-importance and false modesty. It comes from faith-grounded identity — seeing yourself accurately through the lens of who God made you to be.
feedback.incorrect: Sober judgment is not about thinking lowly of yourself (that's false humility) or focusing only on strengths. The session defines it as a balanced view: not too high, not too low, but with clear-eyed sobriety grounded in faith-based identity.

variants.stem: The session describes Paul's concept of 'sober judgment' as the program's 'north star.' What does this concept specifically guard against?
options: Both inflated self-importance and dishonest self-deprecation
Only the sin of pride and arrogance in leadership
Spending too much time on self-reflection instead of serving others
Using secular assessment tools rather than biblical discernment

feedback.correct: Sober judgment is a two-directional guard: it prevents leaders from thinking too highly of themselves AND from being dishonestly self-deprecating. The session emphasizes that both extremes distort the clear-eyed view that comes from faith-grounded identity.
feedback.incorrect: Many people assume sober judgment only corrects pride, but the session explicitly states it works in both directions — 'not more highly than you ought, not less honestly than you should.' False modesty is just as much a failure of sober judgment as arrogance.

questions.stem: In the DVULI Personal Assessment Model, what is the relationship between the three input dimensions (Calling, Character, Commitment to Change) and the three output qualities (Authenticity, Responsiveness, Teachability)?
options: The three dimensions, taken together as an integrated system, produce the three qualities
Each dimension independently produces exactly one corresponding quality in a one-to-one mapping
The three qualities are prerequisites that must develop before the three dimensions can emerge
The dimensions and qualities are interchangeable labels for the same six concepts

feedback.correct: The session explicitly states that the three dimensions 'taken together' produce Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability. This is an integrated system, not a one-to-one mapping. All three dimensions must be present for all three qualities to emerge — they are the marks of a leader who is ready to learn and grow.
feedback.incorrect: The key phrase from the session is 'taken together.' The three dimensions function as an integrated system rather than as independent pipelines. The output qualities are results of the combined dimensions, and the qualities emerge from the system — they are not prerequisites for it.

variants.stem: The DVULI model describes Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability as 'the three marks of the leader who is ready to learn and grow.' How do these qualities originate according to the model?
options: They emerge when Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change work together
They are innate personality traits that some leaders possess naturally
They develop sequentially — first Authenticity, then Responsiveness, then Teachability
They are produced by formal evaluation and feedback from others

feedback.correct: Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability are not personality traits or sequential stages — they are the product of the three dimensions working in concert. When a leader is faithfully called, consistently living their values, and willing to change, these qualities naturally emerge.
feedback.incorrect: The model does not treat these qualities as inborn traits, sequential stages, or products of assessment methods. They are specifically the output of the three foundational dimensions — Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change — operating together.

questions.stem: Classify each statement about the session's teaching on self-assessment and the Personal Assessment Model:

statements.text: Honest self-assessment is a form of worship according to the session's biblical framework
Personal reflection alone is sufficient for uncovering all of a leader's blind spots
Sober judgment means a leader should think of themselves as less capable than they actually are
A leader can demonstrate strong Calling while simultaneously lacking Character
Feedback from trusted community members reveals insights unavailable through self-reflection alone
Commitment to Change requires a leader to abandon all of their current ministry methods and start over

feedback.correct: The key distinctions here involve understanding what sober judgment actually means (accurate, not deflated), recognizing that the three model dimensions are independent (you can have one without another), and appreciating that while community feedback often reveals blind spots, it doesn't automatically do so in every instance.
feedback.incorrect: The most common errors involve treating sober judgment as false humility (it's balanced accuracy), assuming personal reflection can catch blind spots (by definition, you can't easily see them yourself), or thinking Commitment to Change means wholesale reinvention rather than willingness to adjust. Also note that the three model dimensions are distinct — strength in one doesn't guarantee strength in another.

variants.stem: Classify each statement about the session's biblical foundations and methods of self-discovery:

statements.text: Community bears responsibility for helping individuals develop self-knowledge through burden-bearing
Formal evaluations are more reliable than personal reflection for every aspect of self-knowledge
A leader who resists all feedback can still demonstrate full Teachability
The three methods of self-discovery (reflection, feedback, formal evaluation) carry equal weight in every situation
Paul's instruction about sober judgment applies to leaders in ministry contexts
A leader who scores well on formal evaluations has necessarily achieved sober judgment about themselves

feedback.correct: These distinctions test boundary cases. Formal evaluations measure specific constructs but don't guarantee holistic sober judgment. The three methods complement each other without a fixed hierarchy — context determines which is most useful at a given time. Community burden-bearing is always part of the biblical mandate.
feedback.incorrect: Watch for absolute claims. No single method is universally superior. Formal evaluations provide structured data but can't replace relational feedback or personal reflection. And strong evaluation scores don't automatically translate to the kind of sober, integrated self-judgment Paul describes.

questions.stem: The session's two foundational Scripture passages are Galatians 6:2–5 and Romans 12:1–5. Counting both passages together, how many individual Bible verses form the session's scriptural foundation?

feedback.correct: Galatians 6:2–5 spans 4 verses (2, 3, 4, 5) and Romans 12:1–5 spans 5 verses (1, 2, 3, 4, 5), totaling 9 verses. These nine verses provide the complete biblical foundation for the session's teaching on sober judgment, renewing the mind, and burden-bearing community.
feedback.incorrect: Students often undercount by forgetting that both the start and end verse are included (e.g., 6:2–5 is four verses, not three). Others overcount by including surrounding context verses that aren't part of the specified passages. Count carefully: Galatians 6 verses 2 through 5, plus Romans 12 verses 1 through 5.

variants.stem: The DVULI Personal Assessment Model names three foundational input dimensions and three resulting output qualities. How many total named components (dimensions plus qualities combined) make up the complete model?

feedback.correct: The model has 3 input dimensions (Calling, Character, Commitment to Change) and 3 output qualities (Authenticity, Responsiveness, Teachability), totaling 6 named components. Understanding this structure helps you see how the model connects foundational practices to observable leadership qualities.
feedback.incorrect: Students sometimes count only the three dimensions or only the three qualities, arriving at 3. Others add extra elements like 'sober judgment' or the three methods of self-discovery, which are part of the broader session but not components of the Personal Assessment Model itself.

questions.stem: Marcus has been in youth ministry for fifteen years and is widely respected for his passion and impact. When he enters the DVULI program, he completes a formal evaluation that reveals weak communication skills with parents — something his colleagues have mentioned before but he attributed to 'personality differences.' He tells his cohort, 'I know who I am and what God called me to do. I don't need a test to tell me what to fix.' Using the DVULI Personal Assessment Model, the three methods of self-discovery, and the biblical concept of sober judgment, what is the MOST comprehensive assessment of Marcus's situation?
options: Marcus shows strong Calling but deficits in Commitment to Change, as he dismisses both formal evaluation and community feedback — two of the three self-discovery methods — which prevents the sober judgment needed for Teachability
Marcus is a mature leader whose fifteen years of experience have already given him sufficient self-knowledge, making additional assessment unnecessary
Marcus primarily lacks Character because his dismissal of the evaluation shows he is not living out his stated values consistently
Marcus's only issue is that he hasn't found the right formal evaluation tool — once he does, his self-assessment will be complete
justifications: Because the Personal Assessment Model requires all three dimensions working together, and Marcus's refusal to accept insights from formal evaluation and community feedback cuts off two of the three methods of self-discovery, violating Paul's instruction to think with sober judgment rather than an inflated or selective self-image
Because Calling is the only dimension that truly matters in the model, and since Marcus has strong Calling, his resistance to feedback is a minor issue
Because formal evaluations are the most important of the three self-discovery methods, and Marcus's rejection of this single method is the sole cause of his problem
Because sober judgment requires a leader to accept every criticism as true, and Marcus's failure to do so means he lacks all three dimensions of the model

feedback.correct: This scenario requires integrating multiple concepts: Marcus's strong Calling is evident in his 15-year track record, but his dismissal of both colleague feedback and formal evaluation shuts down two of the three self-discovery methods. His Commitment to Change deficit — the unwillingness to adjust thoughts, plans, and actions — prevents the Teachability that only emerges when all three dimensions are present. Paul's sober judgment demands neither inflated self-assessment nor selective hearing but clear-eyed honesty, which Marcus is avoiding.
feedback.incorrect: This question requires synthesizing the Personal Assessment Model (all three dimensions needed), the three methods of self-discovery (Marcus is rejecting two of three), and sober judgment (he is thinking more selectively than he ought). Experience alone doesn't produce self-knowledge, Character is about value-consistency not feedback-acceptance, and no single method is sufficient by itself.
feedback.wrongReason: Students who identify the correct answer but choose the wrong justification often overemphasize one concept — like treating formal evaluation as the primary method or claiming sober judgment means accepting all criticism. The correct justification integrates the model, the methods, and the biblical concept as an interconnected system where each concept reinforces the others.

variants.stem: Priya joined DVULI after a decade leading an after-school tutoring ministry. During the first session, she eagerly completes every formal evaluation and spends hours journaling about her experiences. However, when her small group offers candid feedback that she tends to micromanage volunteers, she becomes defensive and says, 'You don't understand my context — I have to stay in control because the kids depend on me.' Using the DVULI Personal Assessment Model, the three methods of self-discovery, and the biblical concept of sober judgment, what is the MOST comprehensive assessment of Priya's situation?
options: Priya engages with personal reflection and formal evaluation but rejects community feedback — the third method of self-discovery — revealing a Commitment to Change deficit that blocks the Teachability and sober judgment the model requires
Priya is a dedicated leader whose hands-on approach demonstrates strong Character and Calling, making the group's feedback irrelevant
Priya lacks Calling because a truly called leader would never need to micromanage
Priya's engagement with two of three self-discovery methods is sufficient for sober judgment, and the community feedback method is optional
justifications: Because the model requires all three dimensions for Teachability to emerge, and Priya's defensiveness toward community feedback — while embracing personal reflection and formal evaluation — shows she is selectively filtering self-knowledge rather than practicing the sober judgment Paul commands
Because community feedback is the least important of the three self-discovery methods and can be safely ignored when it conflicts with a leader's self-understanding
Because Priya's willingness to journal and complete evaluations proves she has full Commitment to Change regardless of her response to group feedback
Because sober judgment only applies to how leaders view their spiritual gifts, not their management style

feedback.correct: Priya's scenario mirrors Marcus's but with a different pattern: she engages two methods but rejects the third (community feedback). Her defensiveness reveals a selective approach to self-knowledge that violates sober judgment. The model teaches that Teachability requires all three dimensions, and her unwillingness to adjust based on community input indicates a Commitment to Change gap.
feedback.incorrect: No single method of self-discovery is optional or less important. Priya's engagement with reflection and formal tools is commendable but insufficient. The session teaches that blind spots — by their nature — often require outside perspectives. Dismissing community feedback while embracing other methods is a form of selective self-assessment, not sober judgment.
feedback.wrongReason: Students may correctly identify Priya's pattern but justify it by dismissing the importance of community feedback or by claiming that two out of three methods is adequate. The session's framework treats all three methods and all three dimensions as necessary components of an integrated system.

prompts.prompt: Be honest: do you think most people actually know themselves well — or are we all kind of clueless about our own blind spots?
Who knows you better — YOU, or your closest friend? And would you actually want to know what they really think?
If you had to pick ONE thing you're genuinely great at — not just decent at, but your actual best gift — could you name it right now without hesitating?
Which is harder: admitting your flaws to yourself, or hearing them from someone else?
Would you rather get brutally honest feedback about yourself from a stranger, a close friend, or... nobody at all?
Do you think most people are living as their 'true self' — or are they mostly performing a version of themselves they think others want to see?

activities.title: The Sober Judgment Spectrum
activities.description: The instructor opens a live poll (Mentimeter, Poll Everywhere, or the course platform's quiz feature) with 5-7 statements drawn from the session's character and calling categories — e.g., 'I overestimate my patience under pressure,' 'I know my greatest gift and use it intentionally,' 'I am more committed to comfort than to change.' Students rate each statement on a 1-5 scale privately, then results are revealed anonymously as a class heatmap.
