The instructor narrates the patterns aloud: where the group skews high, where they skew low, and what that reveals collectively about blind spots. This becomes a live demonstration of Romans 12:3's 'sober judgment' — not brutal self-criticism, but accurate calibration. Close by asking students to type one word in the chat describing what surprised them about their own answers.
activities.materials: Live polling tool (Mentimeter, Poll Everywhere, or built-in LMS quiz); 5-7 pre-written statements mapped to the Personal Assessment Model
activities.learningGoal: Grounds the abstract concept of 'sober judgment' in immediate, personal self-data and creates shared language for the session
activities.scalingNotes: Works identically at any size since polling is anonymous and aggregated. For very small groups (under 10), skip the heatmap and just have each person share their most surprising rating aloud.

activities.title: Three-Fold Purpose Breakout Rounds
activities.description: Students are split into three breakout rooms of 7-8 people, each room assigned one pillar of the three-fold personal purpose framework: Room A = Knowing God, Room B = Making the World Better, Room C = Using Your Greatest Gift. Each room has one shared discussion prompt posted in the chat: 'What does a person who lives this pillar well actually look like in daily life — and what does neglecting it look like?' Groups have 6 minutes to discuss, then one spokesperson per room gives a 90-second report-back to the full class. The instructor then facilitates a 3-minute synthesis: asking where the three pillars overlap in the students' reports and introducing the idea that authenticity emerges when all three are operating together. Students finish by dropping one sentence in the chat answering: 'Which of these three feels most underdeveloped in your own life right now?'
activities.materials: Breakout room capability (Zoom, Teams, or Google Meet); one discussion prompt per room posted in breakout chat
activities.learningGoal: Forces students to articulate each purpose pillar in concrete behavioral terms rather than abstract ideals, and surfaces personal gaps
activities.scalingNotes: For 25 students, three rooms of ~8 works well. For larger cohorts (40+), run four or five rooms and assign two rooms to the same pillar so report-backs can be compared. For smaller groups (under 15), skip breakouts and run the three prompts as a sequential full-group discussion.

activities.title: The Feedback You Never Asked For
activities.description: The instructor frames this activity around the session's teaching that feedback from others is one of the three primary methods of self-discovery — and that most people only receive useful feedback accidentally. Students are given 3 minutes to silently type answers to two prompts in a private shared doc or the course journal tool: (1) 'Write down the most honest thing someone has ever said about you that you initially rejected but later recognized as true.' (2) 'Write down one thing people consistently thank you for or come to you about — even if you don't take it seriously.' Students are then paired in 2-minute breakout rooms to read one of their answers aloud to their partner, who reflects back one observation. The full group reconvenes and the instructor asks for 3-4 volunteers to share what the exercise revealed — not the content of the feedback, but what it felt like to say it out loud. Closes with a brief teaching moment on the difference between feedback that attacks identity and feedback that reveals calling.
activities.materials: Private journaling space in course app or shared doc link; breakout room capability for pairs
activities.learningGoal: Activates the 'feedback from others' discovery method and helps students distinguish between defensive self-protection and genuine teachability (the T in the ART model)
activities.scalingNotes: Pairs work cleanly at 25 students. If attendance is uneven, one group of three is fine — the third person observes and affirms. For asynchronous versions, post the two prompts as a discussion board thread where students respond to at least one peer.

activities.title: Calling + Character + Commitment Audit
activities.description: The instructor shares a pre-built collaborative template (Google Slides, Miro, or Jamboard) with 25 identical canvas tiles — one per student, already labeled with their names or participant numbers. Each tile has three boxes: Calling (what I seem built for), Character (where I am vs. where I need to be), and Commitment to Change (one specific shift I'm willing to make this week). Students have 7 minutes to silently fill in their own tile. The instructor then opens a structured gallery walk in the chat: students are asked to find TWO other tiles and leave one affirming observation or question in the comments on each. The instructor monitors the board live, calls out 2-3 patterns they notice across the tiles, and asks the full group: 'What does it tell us that so many of us named similar callings but different character gaps?' Close by having students privately circle the one word on their tile that feels most true and most unsettling at the same time.
activities.materials: Pre-built collaborative canvas (Google Slides, Miro, Jamboard, or FigJam) with 25 labeled tiles shared via link before or at session start
activities.learningGoal: Applies the full Personal Assessment Model (Calling + Character + Commitment to Change) in a single integrated exercise and introduces peer observation as a accountability structure
activities.scalingNotes: The tile-based format scales from 10 to 50 without changes. For asynchronous cohorts, the same template can be completed over 48 hours with comment-based peer feedback replacing the live gallery walk.

activities.title: Burden Board: What Do You Carry Without Being Asked?
activities.description: The instructor introduces the biblical concept of burden-bearing community (Galatians 6:2) as a diagnostic tool — the burdens we naturally carry for others often point directly toward calling. The instructor opens a shared collaborative whiteboard (Jamboard, Padlet, or Miro) with two columns: 'Burdens I carry without being asked' and 'Burdens others bring to me.' Students have 4 minutes to add sticky notes to both columns anonymously. The instructor then narrates the board live, grouping themes as they emerge (justice, teaching, emotional care, logistics, creative problems, etc.) and asks: 'Notice any category you keep contributing to — that's data about your greatest gift, not a coincidence.' Students finish by typing in the chat: one burden from the board that they didn't expect to see so many others share, and one they're surprised they carry alone. This reframes burden-bearing from exhaustion into vocation.
activities.materials: Collaborative whiteboard tool (Padlet, Jamboard, Miro, or FigJam) with two labeled columns; shared link distributed before activity begins
activities.learningGoal: Connects the burden-bearing community concept to the three-fold framework's 'making the world better' pillar and surfaces calling data through relational patterns rather than self-reflection alone
activities.scalingNotes: Anonymous sticky notes work at any size. For larger groups (40+), add a third column for 'Burdens I avoid entirely' to generate richer contrast. For async delivery, run this as a 72-hour Padlet with a follow-up prompt asking students to respond to one peer's note.

activities.title: The Three Lenses Debrief: Experience, Others, Evaluation
activities.description: This closing synthesis activity asks students to apply all three self-discovery methods — personal experience, feedback from others, and formal evaluation — to one specific question about themselves. The instructor posts a single prompt in the chat: 'Think of one quality, tendency, or pattern that keeps showing up in your life. How does each lens see it differently?' Students are given 4 minutes to write privately, then sorted into breakout rooms of 4-5 where each person shares their pattern and which of the three lenses gave them the clearest picture. Groups are asked to identify: which lens did most members trust the most — and which did they tend to avoid? A spokesperson reports back one insight per group. The instructor closes by teaching the key distinction: no single lens gives the full picture, and a person with ruthless self-knowledge is one who keeps all three active rather than defaulting to the most comfortable one. Students end by posting in chat which of the three lenses they're committing to strengthen.
activities.materials: Breakout room capability; optional shared note-taking doc for group reporters
activities.learningGoal: Integrates all three methods of self-discovery into a single personal case study and reinforces that authentic self-knowledge is a multi-source, ongoing practice rather than a one-time insight
activities.scalingNotes: Groups of 4-5 work well for 25 students (five groups). For smaller classes, run as a single full-group discussion with the instructor facilitating each lens in sequence. For asynchronous delivery, turn the prompt into a structured reflection journal entry with peer comment requirements.

slides.title: Who Am I? The Biblical Case for Ruthless Self-Knowledge
slides.bodyText: Most people know their coffee order better than they know themselves — and the Bible has something to say about that.
slides.speakerNotes: Welcome everyone to Session 1. This chapter sets the foundation for everything that follows. The question 'Who am I?' sounds simple — almost too simple for a serious course. But we're going to argue today that honest, rigorous self-knowledge is not just psychologically useful — it's a spiritual discipline. Tease the arc: we'll look at WHY self-knowledge matters, a three-part model for assessing yourself, and three concrete methods for doing it. Let the hook land before you move on.

slides.title: The Mirror Problem
slides.bodyText: We're about to look at why most people see themselves incorrectly — and why Scripture demands we do better.
slides.speakerNotes: Transition into the problem. Ask the room: 'How many of you have ever been surprised by feedback you received about yourself — positive OR negative?' Almost everyone will raise a hand. That gap between self-perception and reality is exactly what this section is about. Introduce the idea that self-ignorance isn't just a personality flaw — it has real consequences for how we lead, relate, and serve.

slides.title: Romans 12:3
slides.bodyText: "Do not think of yourself more highly than you ought, but rather think of yourself with sober judgment, in accordance with the faith God has distributed to each of you."
slides.speakerNotes: Read the verse slowly. Note the phrase 'sober judgment' — this is a medical metaphor. Sober as opposed to intoxicated. Paul is saying self-perception can be distorted, inflated, or numbed — just like someone who's had too much to drink. The antidote isn't self-deprecation; it's accuracy. Ask students: 'What does it feel like to be around someone who has clearly sober self-knowledge versus someone who doesn't?' Let two or three people respond before moving on.

slides.title: The Core Argument
slides.bodyText: You cannot give what you haven't first found — and ruthless self-knowledge is how you find it.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the thesis of the entire chapter. Pause after showing this slide. Let it sit for 5 seconds. Then say: 'Every great leader, every faithful servant, every person who made a real dent in the world — they did it by knowing their gifts, their limits, and their purpose with unusual clarity.' Ask students to jot down one area where they feel genuinely unclear about who they are right now. They'll use this later.

slides.title: Why You Exist: A Three-Fold Framework
bullets: Know God — relationship before function
Make the world better — purpose is always outward-facing
Use your greatest gift — uniqueness is stewardship, not pride
slides.speakerNotes: Walk through each of the three with a brief example. For 'Know God': this is first — if purpose is disconnected from relationship, it becomes mere performance. For 'Make the world better': ask students to think of someone who made their world better — what did that person know about themselves that enabled it? For 'Greatest gift': stress that this isn't arrogance — refusing to identify and deploy your greatest gift is actually a form of burying your talent (Matthew 25). This is the 'why' before the 'how.'

slides.title: The ARC Assessment Model
slides.bodyText: Three questions about who you are lead to three qualities that define how you show up.
slides.speakerNotes: Signal a major shift here. We've talked about WHY self-knowledge matters. Now we're building a framework for WHAT to assess. Tease the three C's: Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change. Tell students: 'These aren't three separate boxes — they interact. And the output isn't just insight, it's a way of being in the world.' Build anticipation before the next slides unpack each piece.

slides.title: The Three C's: What to Assess
bullets: Calling — What am I uniquely wired to do?
Character — Who am I when no one is watching?
Commitment to Change — Am I willing to be reformed by what I find?
slides.speakerNotes: Spend real time on each. Calling: not just career, but deep wiring — strengths, passions, the intersection of what you're good at and what energizes you. Character: this is the hardest one to assess honestly because we all have blind spots and defenses. Commitment to Change: point out that many people pursue self-assessment as a mirror they admire rather than a window to walk through. The third C is what separates curiosity from transformation. Ask: 'Which of these three do you find hardest to assess honestly?' Take a quick show of hands.

slides.title: Three C's → Three Qualities
bullets: Calling → Authenticity
Character → Responsiveness
Commitment to Change → Teachability
slides.bodyText: ["You're not performing a role — you're living from the inside out", "Your character shapes how others experience you, moment to moment", "Growth isn't optional — it's the point"]
slides.speakerNotes: Walk this comparison carefully. Left column is INPUT — what you're assessing. Right column is OUTPUT — who you become when that assessment is done honestly. Authenticity isn't just 'being real' — it's the result of knowing and accepting your calling without apology or inflation. Responsiveness is the fruit of honest character work — people who know their own patterns respond rather than react. Teachability is the most observable quality — you can usually tell within minutes whether someone is teachable. Ask: 'Who's the most teachable person you know? What makes them that way?'

slides.title: The Goal of Self-Knowledge
slides.bodyText: The point is not self-awareness as an end — it's ART: Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability as a way of life.
slides.speakerNotes: Drive home the ART acronym here. Write it on the board if you can. The danger of self-assessment culture is navel-gazing — people who are endlessly fascinated with themselves but never change. The biblical model is different: self-knowledge in service of God and others. Authenticity so your relationships aren't performances. Responsiveness so you're shaped by truth. Teachability so growth never stops. Invite a student to share one person they've known who embodied all three.

slides.title: Three Paths to Self-Discovery
slides.bodyText: Scripture doesn't just tell us to know ourselves — it gives us a community and a set of practices for doing it.
slides.speakerNotes: Transition to the 'how.' Note that the last section answered WHAT to assess (the three C's). This section answers HOW. The three methods — personal reflection, feedback from others, and formal evaluation — correspond roughly to how the Bible talks about wisdom: personal meditation, communal accountability, and structured testing. Ask: 'Which of these three do you already do naturally? Which do you avoid?'

slides.title: Method 1: Reflecting on Personal Experience
bullets: Review your highs — when did you feel most alive and effective?
Review your lows — what patterns show up in your failures?
Look for recurring themes across seasons and roles
Journaling, prayer, and silence create space for honest reflection
slides.speakerNotes: Personal experience is the most accessible method but often the least systematic. Most people haven't actually reviewed their life narrative looking for patterns. Suggest the 'peak experiences' exercise: think of three moments in your life when you felt most fully yourself. What were you doing? What gift was in use? What value was being expressed? Point out that Romans 12:2 ('renewing of the mind') implies an active, ongoing process of re-examination, not a one-time insight.

slides.title: Galatians 6:2
slides.bodyText: "Carry each other's burdens, and in this way you will fulfill the law of Christ." — The community that sees you clearly is the community that helps you grow.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the biblical grounding for Method 2: feedback from others. The idea of burden-bearing extends to the burden of blind spots — things you cannot see about yourself that others can. Point out that this requires a specific kind of community: people who know you well, who have permission to speak honestly, and who have your growth as their goal. Ask: 'Do you have one person in your life right now who has permission to tell you hard truths?' This is often a convicting moment. Don't rush past it.

slides.title: Methods 2 & 3: Feedback and Formal Evaluation
bullets: Method 2 — Trusted community: seek people who know you, not just like you
Ask specific questions — 'What do you see me doing when I'm at my best?'
Method 3 — Formal tools: assessments create shared language for self-discovery
No tool is definitive — use them as mirrors, not cages
slides.speakerNotes: For feedback: emphasize the difference between people who affirm us and people who actually help us see. Both are valuable but in different ways — self-knowledge requires the latter. Give the example of a 360-degree review in a work context — it's uncomfortable precisely because it bypasses our defenses. For formal evaluation: mention personality inventories, strengths assessments, spiritual gifts surveys. The danger is using these as identity labels ('I'm an INTJ, that's just how I am') rather than conversation starters. The goal is insight, not excuse.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: Sober self-judgment is a biblical command, not a self-help trend
Purpose has three dimensions: knowing God, improving the world, and deploying your gift
Calling + Character + Commitment to Change produce Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability
Personal reflection, trusted community, and formal tools are three concrete paths inward
Self-knowledge is not the destination — faithful, fruitful living is
slides.speakerNotes: Land the plane here. Ask students to look back at what they wrote at the start — the area where they feel most unclear about who they are. Which of the three methods would help them most in that area? Assign: before next session, use ONE of the three methods intentionally. Either journal about a peak experience, ask one trusted person one honest question, or take one formal assessment. Close with a brief prayer or reflection prompt: 'God, show me myself as you see me — not to paralyze me, but to free me.'

blocks.title: Audiobook
blocks.transcript:
She had led a youth ministry for eleven years. She had seen young people walk away from gangs and walk toward diplomas. She had lost volunteers, buried teenagers, wept in church parking lots at midnight, and preached hope when she felt none. She had given everything she had. And then, on the first morning of the DeVos Urban Leadership Initiative's first local workshop, a facilitator asked a question that stopped her cold.

"Don't tell me what you do," the facilitator said. "Tell us — who are you?"

The room went quiet. She opened her mouth and closed it again. In eleven years of ministry, no one had ever asked her that. More unsettling still: she realized, sitting in that circle, that she did not have a ready answer. She knew her program. She knew her outcomes. She knew her board members' names and her at-risk youth's phone numbers. But who she was — her strengths, her blind spots, the patterns of behavior that shaped every room she walked into — that knowledge was thin. She had been so busy doing the work that she had never stopped long enough to understand the worker.

This session is your formal invitation to stop. To look clearly — not more highly than you ought, but not less honestly than you should — at the person God has created you to be. What you discover in this space will form the root system for everything else the D-V-U-L-I program is designed to grow in you.

[short pause]

Leadership development programs that skip the interior work — that jump straight to strategies, competencies, and skill-building — are building on sand. The most sophisticated tool in the hands of a leader who does not know himself or herself will, sooner or later, become an instrument of harm rather than help. This is not a harsh verdict; it is an observed reality. Burnout, isolation, and leadership failure in urban ministry contexts are almost never primarily the result of insufficient technical skills. They are, more often, the result of leaders who never developed an honest, grounded, and faith-integrated account of who they are. That finding comes from the work of Bruce Avolio in 2005, and it has been confirmed repeatedly since.

The DVULI program calls the destination of this interior work DIVINE DESIGN — the process of understanding who God has created you to be and what he has called and equipped you to do. But divine design cannot be found by looking outward alone, toward programs and plans and community needs. It must begin with the kind of inward honest reckoning that the Apostle Paul calls SOBER JUDGMENT — a phrase we will unpack carefully as we go.

Researcher Tasha Eurich's multi-year investigation into self-awareness found that only ten to fifteen percent of people actually possess the quality they claim. Most people, her research concludes, dramatically overestimate how clearly they see themselves. The consequences of this gap are not trivial: leaders with low self-awareness consistently make poorer decisions, build weaker teams, and cause more relational damage — usually while remaining genuinely convinced that the problem lies elsewhere. That is Eurich's finding from 2017, and the invitation to honest self-knowledge that flows from it is not an academic exercise. It is a prerequisite for sustainable, healthy, effective ministry leadership.

[short pause]

The DVULI program grounds its entire approach to personal assessment in two passages of Scripture that are worth taking slowly and carefully, because they are doing more work than they might initially appear to do.

The first comes from Paul's letter to the Galatians, chapter six, verses two through five: "Carry each other's burdens, and in this way you will fulfill the law of Christ. If anyone thinks they are something when they are not, they deceive themselves. Each one should test their own actions. Then they can take pride in themselves alone, without comparing themselves to someone else, for each one should carry their own load."

The second comes from Romans, chapter twelve, verses one through five: "Do not conform to the pattern of this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind. Then you will be able to test and approve what God's will is — his good, pleasing and perfect will. For by the grace given me I say to every one of you: Do not think of yourself more highly than you ought, but rather think of yourself with sober judgment, in accordance with the faith God has distributed to each of you. For just as each of us has one body with many members, and these members do not all have the same function, so in Christ we, though many, form one body, and each member belongs to all the others."

[short pause]

The first passage, from Galatians, establishes a crucial paradox at the heart of Christian community: you are simultaneously called to carry each other's burdens and to carry your own load. This is not a contradiction — it is a precise description of mature, differentiated community life. You cannot be a genuine burden-bearer for others unless you first know clearly what your own load looks like. The leader who has never honestly assessed her own strength, weakness, blind spots, and breaking points will, in crisis moments, either collapse under others' burdens or unconsciously transfer her own unprocessed weight onto those she is trying to serve. That is a pattern Banks and Ledbetter identified in 2004, and it plays out with painful regularity across ministry contexts.

Notice also what Paul says about self-deception: "If anyone thinks they are something when they are not, they deceive themselves." The Greek word for "test" in this passage — dokimazō — is the same word used for assaying metal: the process of heating an ore to determine its true composition. Honest self-assessment, in Paul's theology, is not ego-driven introspection. It is the act of submitting one's life to the refining heat of truthful examination so that what is genuine can be distinguished from what is merely imagined.

The second passage, from Romans twelve, introduces the phrase that functions as DVULI's north star for this entire session: think of yourself with sober judgment. Banks and Ledbetter argue persuasively that this phrase is neither a call to false modesty nor to self-promotion. Paul is calling for something far more demanding — what we might translate as clear-eyed, faith-grounded sobriety about who you actually are. Not more highly than you ought. Not less honestly than you should. But with the uncommon clarity that comes from seeing yourself through the lens of God's grace rather than through the distorting mirrors of ego protection or shame.

And crucially, Romans twelve situates this self-knowledge within the body of Christ. You are not called to assess yourself in isolation, comparing yourself to your own private benchmark. You are called to understand yourself as a member of a body — uniquely gifted, uniquely called, uniquely placed. The self-knowledge that Romans twelve invites is inherently communal: it is self-knowledge in service of the whole body, and it is best developed in community with that body. This is why the DVULI city group — the community of peers you are beginning this journey with — is not merely a learning convenience. It is a theological necessity. The journey cannot be traveled alone.

[short pause]

Before moving on, take a moment to consider: when was the last time someone who knew you well offered you an honest assessment of your strengths or blind spots — and you genuinely received it? What made that possible, or what made it difficult? How might a community intentionally committed to sober judgment look different from ordinary organizational feedback?

[short pause]

The DVULI personal assessment process begins with purpose — not because purpose questions are easy, but because they are foundational. Before you can understand the dimensions of who you are, you need a framework for understanding what you are for. The program draws on Richard Bolles' landmark work, "What Color Is Your Parachute?", published in 2021, which introduces a three-part framework for personal mission that has profoundly shaped vocational discernment curricula for decades.

As Dik and Duffy observed in 2012, a sense of calling — of having a transcendent purpose that guides one's work — is not reserved for clergy or extraordinary individuals. It is available to any person through a process of intentional self-reflection, feedback, and community discernment. The three-fold framework from Bolles gives that process a structural home. Let's explore each dimension.

The FIRST PURPOSE is to know God, and enjoy him forever. This is not a pious preface to the "real" parts of the framework. It is, in the deepest sense, the entire point. Every other dimension of divine design flows from the quality of a leader's relationship with the living God. A youth ministry that is built on techniques, passion, and community need — but not on an ongoing, deepening encounter with the God who called the leader to that work — will eventually run dry. The reservoir is the relationship. DVULI's commitment to mentoring, journaling, reflective practice, and prayerful community is grounded precisely in this conviction: that sustainable ministry leadership requires a leader who is actively, honestly, and joyfully in relationship with God.

As the DVULI workbook frames it: our purpose doesn't change, but our understanding and growth in it may. As our roles or settings change, the way we express this purpose may stay the same, shift, or expand. The question "Where are you in relation to this purpose?" is not a test to pass. It is a diagnostic — a gentle pressure point that reveals where growth is needed and where gratitude is warranted.

[short pause]

The SECOND PURPOSE is to make the world a better place — to do what you can, moment by moment, day by day, step by step, to make this world a better place, following the leading and guidance of God's Spirit. This is your vocational purpose. It is prosocial at its core. Dik and Duffy identified this prosocial orientation in 2009 as one of three essential dimensions of calling: a transcendent summons, meaningful work, and the drive to contribute to something beyond oneself. The leader who has genuinely internalized this second purpose cannot be primarily motivated by personal advancement, institutional preservation, or the applause of stakeholders. She is compelled, at the deepest level, by love for the community she serves.

Note the temporal humility built into Bolles' language: moment by moment, day by day, step by step. The second purpose is not a destination to arrive at — it is a process to inhabit. Your purpose becomes clearer over time as you implement it. It deepens as you see it through different perspectives. This is one reason the DVULI program does not expect participants to arrive at the first local workshop with a polished, final purpose statement. You are expected to arrive honestly — to bring where you actually are, not where you think you should be.

[short pause]

The THIRD PURPOSE is the most individual: to exercise the talent you particularly came to earth to use — your greatest gift, which you most delight to use, in the place God has designed for you, and for the purposes God most needs done in the world. Bolles calls this your "Flower" — the intersection of your peak skills, your deepest values, the settings that energize you, and the problems you feel most compelled to solve.

Bill George, in 2003, calls this a leader's TRUE NORTH — the internal compass built through experience, reflection, and honest self-assessment, and that orients every major leadership decision. The leader who has done the work of identifying her greatest gift is not easily seduced by ministry opportunities that look important but are actually a poor fit. She can say no to good things because she is clear about what she is for. She can absorb criticism without being shattered and receive affirmation without being inflated, because her identity is anchored not in performance or reputation, but in the clear-eyed knowledge of what God has uniquely equipped her to do.

These three purposes — knowing God, making the world better, and using your greatest gift — interlock to form a complete personal purpose statement grounded in faith, service, and unique gifting. None of them stands alone. Together, they form the foundation for everything that follows.

[short pause]

With the three-fold purpose framework providing the "why" of your leadership, the DVULI Personal Assessment Model provides the "who." It is the program's primary diagnostic framework for understanding the leader as a whole person — not merely as a skill set or a role, but as an integrated human being whose effectiveness in ministry is inseparable from her interior life, her values, and her willingness to grow.

The model is organized around three foundational dimensions and three resulting qualities that emerge when those dimensions are integrated. This is not a static inventory of personality traits. It is a dynamic map of the capacities required to lead, learn, and serve sustainably over time.

The first core dimension is CALLING — defined in the DVULI framework as "faithfully doing the unique work that God has called and equipped me to do as my contribution to building his Kingdom." Calling, in this sense, is more than career satisfaction or vocational interest. As Dik and Duffy described in 2009, genuine calling encompasses a transcendent summons — a sense of being called from beyond oneself — purposeful, meaning-making activity, and a prosocial motivation oriented toward the good of others. The DVULI definition aligns precisely with all three of these dimensions. Calling is not what you do when you have time. It is the central organizing principle of your leadership life — the work you were uniquely made for, and that the Kingdom most needs you to do.

The second core dimension is CHARACTER — defined as "consistently living out the values I espouse, applying them to my every action and decision." Character, in this framework, is not simply about avoiding moral failure. It is about the alignment — or misalignment — between what a leader professes and what she actually practices. Peterson and Seligman, in their landmark classification of character strengths and virtues published in 2004, argue that character is both stable and developable: virtues can be identified, assessed, and cultivated through intentional practice. When DVULI asks whether you are "consistently living out the values you espouse," it is pressing on the question of integration: are the values displayed on your ministry's website the same values driving your decisions at eleven at night when no one is watching?

The third core dimension is COMMITMENT TO CHANGE — defined as "willingly adjusting my thoughts, plans, and actions to align them more fully with God's will for my life and ministry." This dimension is, in many respects, the hardest of the three. Calling can be energizing. Character can feel like natural virtue. But commitment to change requires the leader to hold every assumption, every strategy, every familiar way of doing things in an open hand — willing to release what is comfortable in favor of what is faithful. Bill George describes this quality as the opposite of what he calls "imposter syndrome" — the tendency to project a confident, settled self-image while privately refusing to examine the gaps between aspiration and reality. The leader with genuine commitment to change welcomes the discomfort of honest feedback precisely because she values alignment with God's will over the comfort of self-protection.

[short pause]

When these three dimensions are integrated, the DVULI model identifies three qualities that mark the leader who is genuinely ready to learn and grow. These are not additional competencies to acquire — they are the natural fruit of calling, character, and commitment to change working together.

The first resulting quality is AUTHENTICITY — produced by the integration of calling and character — and it is defined as "integrating both doing what God calls me to do and being who God has designed me to be." As Bill George argued in 2003, authentic leadership begins precisely here, in the convergence of purpose and integrity. The authentic leader is not performing a role. She is operating from the deepest truth of who she is. When calling and character are integrated, the leader's outward ministry and inward life reinforce rather than contradict each other. There is no performance gap — no exhausting distance between the person people see and the person who lies awake at night.

The second resulting quality is RESPONSIVENESS — produced by calling and commitment to change — defined as "having the attitude of Christ: being willing to submit my will to God's will, as modeled by Jesus in his responsiveness to people, circumstances, and most importantly, his Father's will." The responsive leader is not reactive in the sense of being blown about by every wind of circumstance. She is responsive in the deeper sense of remaining open — to the Spirit's leading, to the community's need, to the invitation to revise her plans in light of God's larger purposes. This is the quality that keeps vocational leaders from becoming prisoners of their own previous commitments.

The third resulting quality is TEACHABILITY — produced by character and commitment to change — defined as "wanting to be transformed through the renewing of my mind: being a learner rather than a knower." This is the quality that makes the entire DVULI experience possible. A leader who enters the program already certain of what she knows — about urban ministry, about her community, about herself — will extract relatively little from the learning opportunities ahead. A leader who enters as a genuine learner, willing to have her assumptions examined and her certainties tested, will be transformed. Eurich's research in 2017 identifies teachability — the willingness to seek and receive truthful feedback from multiple sources — as one of the most consistent predictors of high self-awareness. It is no coincidence that DVULI places it at the very foundation of the learning journey.

[short pause]

The DVULI pre-assignment identifies three distinct methods through which leaders can gain genuine self-knowledge. What is crucial to understand is that none of these methods is complete by itself. Each illuminates a different dimension of who you are, and each has characteristic blind spots that the others help correct. The leader who relies on only one method is like a person who insists on navigating by only one star — likely to drift off course without ever realizing it.

The first method is reflecting on personal experience. The DVULI framework describes this as "the most useful source, provided you have a clear framework to guide your reflection." The qualification is important. Unguided introspection — the kind of circular self-examination that goes over the same ground again and again without deepening understanding — is not what the program intends. Douglas Rumford, whose writing on journaling is included in the DVULI pre-assignment materials, describes the difference between a diary and a genuine learning journal: the learning journal is "a place where you reflect intelligently on what and how you are learning, including how you are applying these lessons and what outcomes you are achieving."

Eurich offers an important caution about introspection that DVULI takes seriously: research shows that introspection can actually decrease accuracy rather than increase it, if the introspection is focused on the wrong questions. The question "Why do I do this?" often leads to confabulation — the construction of plausible-sounding explanations that feel true but are not. The more productive question is "What?" — What patterns do I notice in my behavior? What circumstances seem to activate my best and worst responses? What have I learned from the experiences of the past year? The reflection exercises in the pre-assignment are designed to channel personal reflection toward exactly these productive questions.

[short pause]

The second method is receiving honest feedback from others. This addresses the fundamental limitation of all purely internal self-assessment: you cannot see yourself the way others see you. Eurich distinguishes between internal self-awareness — how clearly you see your own values, passions, and patterns — and external self-awareness — how accurately you understand how others experience you. High performers in both dimensions are consistently better leaders, decision-makers, and relationship-builders. But the two forms of self-awareness are not automatically correlated: you can have a great deal of self-knowledge in one dimension while being almost completely blind in the other.

This is why the DVULI city group is structured to provide ongoing, honest, constructive feedback throughout the program. The core values mentor relationship is designed for the same purpose. Paul's instruction to "carry each other's burdens" is not only about crisis support; it is about the everyday work of seeing each other clearly and speaking what we see with love. The program builds in multiple structured feedback opportunities precisely because the leaders who most need an honest mirror are often the ones least likely to seek one voluntarily.

[short pause]

The third method is formal evaluation. This provides the kind of standardized, validated, comparative data that personal reflection and peer feedback cannot generate on their own. The DVULI pre-assignment introduces two formal tools: the Biblical D-I-S-C Assessment, which helps identify patterns in behavioral style and communication preference, and the Leadership Strategy Assessment — the L-S-A — which maps preferred leadership strategies across four key approaches: Instructing, Developing, Mentoring, and Commissioning.

The purpose of these tools is not to put participants in boxes or reduce complex human beings to a graph pattern. The DVULI workbook is explicit: there is no one "right" style. Rather, formal evaluation provides a shared vocabulary for discussing behavioral patterns — including both strengths and potential overuse — in ways that can catalyze genuine insight and productive community conversation. Peterson and Seligman's work in 2004 similarly demonstrates that naming and measuring one's strengths — rather than focusing exclusively on deficits — produces significantly greater growth and resilience over time.

All three methods are necessary. None is complete alone. The pre-assignment puts it plainly: "Consider them all as you grow in your own self-awareness." It is worth asking yourself honestly: which of these three methods have you used least in the past year? The answer likely reveals something important about where your next growth edge lies.

[short pause]

The DVULI program uses the image of a leadership tree as a powerful way to bring together everything you are discovering in this session. The tree's roots represent your purpose — the invisible but essential foundation that determines whether the whole tree will be able to sustain weight, weather storms, and bear fruit over time. The trunk represents the people who support you — the relationships without which no leader can thrive in the long term. And the branches and leaves represent what is visible to others — your behavioral style, your leadership strategies, your observable patterns of action.

A healthy tree can live for a very long time, but one with damaged or underdeveloped parts soon dies. Likewise, a leader is only effective when she uses all parts of her divine design together to create a strong root system, a sturdy trunk, and a full set of branches. This is why the personal assessment process does not stop at purpose — it moves through behavioral style, leadership strategy, and community formation before the first local workshop concludes. You are not simply completing exercises. You are building the architecture of a leadership life that can last.

As you prepare to complete your leadership tree, ask yourself: which part of your tree feels strongest right now — roots, trunk, or branches? Which part is most in need of development? What would it mean for your ministry if you invested intentional energy there this year?

[short pause]

Avolio argues that sustainable leader development must begin with identity — with a leader's deeply held sense of who she is, what she values, and how she is experienced by others. His concept of developmental readiness — the degree to which a leader is motivated to learn and genuinely capable of self-examination — maps directly onto what DVULI calls teachability. The leaders who gain the most from programs like this are not the most polished or experienced. They are the ones who arrive the most genuinely open — ready to be surprised, challenged, and changed by what they find in the mirror of honest self-knowledge.

[short pause]

We began this session with a question that stopped a seasoned ministry leader cold: Who are you? It is worth returning to that question now — not to answer it definitively, because you will be working on that answer for the rest of this program, and in some sense for the rest of your life — but to understand what is at stake in taking it seriously.

Paul's instruction in Romans twelve is deliberately embedded in a passage about worship. "Offer your bodies as a living sacrifice," he writes — and then, in the next breath, he calls for the renewal of the mind and sober self-judgment. This is not a coincidence. In Paul's theology, honest self-assessment is not a therapeutic exercise or a professional development activity. It is a form of worship. To see yourself clearly — your strengths, your gifts, your calling, your character gaps, your behavioral patterns, your blind spots — and to offer that honest seeing back to God as the starting point for a life of obedient service: this is what the renewed mind looks like in practice.

The leader who refuses to do this work — who hides behind busyness, institutional role, or the flattery of those who depend on her — is not merely missing a development opportunity. She is, in Paul's language, "deceiving herself." She is offering God a performance rather than a person. The reward for stepping out in faith on a learning journey, as the DVULI pre-assignment puts it, is that you will know who you are at your best — the unique person God has created you to be, and the unique work for which he has called and equipped you. That reward is worth the discomfort of the question. In fact, no other starting point will do.

[short pause]

To pull together the key threads of this session: honest self-knowledge is not narcissism — it is a form of worship and a prerequisite for genuine burden-bearing community, rooted in Romans twelve and Galatians six. Paul's call to sober judgment means seeing yourself neither more highly than you ought nor less honestly than you should — through the clear lens of faith-grounded identity. The three-fold purpose framework from Bolles provides the foundation for understanding what you are for: to know God, to make the world better, and to use your greatest gift in the place God has designed for you. The DVULI Personal Assessment Model then organizes leadership identity around three core dimensions — calling, character, and commitment to change — which together produce authenticity, responsiveness, and teachability. Self-knowledge is built through three complementary methods — personal reflection, feedback from others, and formal evaluation — none of which is complete without the other two. And the D-I-S-C and Leadership Strategy Assessment tools are not boxes to fit into; they are shared vocabulary tools for understanding behavioral patterns and engaging in productive community conversation. Developmental readiness — the genuine willingness to learn, grow, and be changed — is the most important quality you bring to this journey.

[short pause]

The next session builds on the foundation laid here by bringing your assessment data into community conversation. Having completed your D-I-S-C and Leadership Strategy Assessment reports, you will explore what your behavioral style and leadership strategy patterns mean for how you show up in ministry — and, crucially, how your patterns interact with those of the colleagues sitting across from you in the city group. The question will shift from "Who am I?" to "Who are we together — and what can we build because of our differences?"

blocks.title: Infographic
blocks.prompt: Create a polished educational infographic in 16:9 landscape format with a light cream background (#faf8f5) titled "The Path to Ruthless Self-Knowledge" in bold dark navy (#0f172a) at the top center, with a smaller subtitle beneath in italic gray text reading "A Biblical Framework for Honest Self-Assessment." The infographic has three main visual zones arranged left to right. ON THE LEFT, a vertical column labeled "YOUR THREE-FOLD PURPOSE" in small uppercase #00558C letters, containing three stacked rounded rectangles with icons: a glowing compass icon with the label "Know God," a globe with sprouting leaves icon labeled "Make the World Better," and a diamond/gem icon labeled "Use Your Greatest Gift," all three boxes connected by a thin vertical line, each box white with a subtle #00558C left border. IN THE CENTER, the largest section, show the "Personal Assessment Model" as a triangular diagram with three nodes at each corner: "CALLING" at the top in a #00558C circle, "CHARACTER" at the bottom-left in a #319B41 circle, and "COMMITMENT TO CHANGE" at the bottom-right in a #d97706 circle, with arrows connecting all three nodes to each other. In the very center of the triangle, place a radiant core labeled "AUTHENTIC SELF" in bold. From this central core, three small arrows radiate outward to three pill-shaped badges labeled "Authenticity," "Responsiveness," and "Teachability" in white text on #319B41 backgrounds. Below the triangle, include a small banner quoting "Think of yourself with sober judgment — Romans 12:3" in italic dark gray text. ON THE RIGHT, a vertical column labeled "THREE METHODS OF DISCOVERY" in small uppercase #00558C letters, showing three horizontal rows each with a distinct icon: a mirror/reflection icon labeled "Reflect on Personal Experience," a speech bubble with people icon labeled "Feedback from Others," and a clipboard with checkmark icon labeled "Formal Evaluation," each row having a brief one-line descriptor beneath in small muted gray text reading respectively "What has your life taught you?", "What do trusted voices say?", and "What do assessments reveal?" Connect the left purpose column and the right methods column to the central triangle with subtle dotted curved arrows in light gray, suggesting they feed into the model. Use clean sans-serif typography throughout, with section headers in #00558C, a cohesive palette of #00558C primary, #319B41 secondary, #d97706 warm accent, and white card elements with soft drop shadows against the #faf8f5 background. Keep all text labels short and readable. Style is modern, clean, minimal, professional educational infographic with flat icons and geometric shapes.

config.description: An interactive three-panel accordion widget. Each panel represents one of the three purposes (Know God / Make the World Better / Use Your Greatest Gift). Inside each panel, participants respond to one guided reflection prompt via a text area, then drag a slider from 'Just Beginning' to 'Living This Fully' to indicate where they currently are on that dimension. When all three sliders are set, a visual 'Purpose Compass' populates showing the overall shape of their current purpose engagement — revealing which dimensions are strong and which need attention. Participants can click each slider position to see a brief Scripture-grounded encouragement.
config.rationale: The slider-plus-text combination forces both felt assessment and verbal articulation simultaneously, activating dual encoding and metacognitive awareness before the session delivers conceptual content — establishing a 'predict before you peek' entry point into the three-fold mission.

blocks.title: The Personal Assessment Model Hexagon Explorer

config.concept: Personal Assessment Model: Calling + Character + Commitment to Change
config.description: An interactive SVG hexagon with six labeled nodes: Calling, Character, Commitment to Change, Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability. Participants click each node to reveal the DVULI definition, a short vignette of what that dimension looks like in practice, and a self-assessment slider (1–5). The three outer nodes (Authenticity, Responsiveness, Teachability) visually dim until the three core nodes are completed. Clicking connecting lines between nodes displays the formula (Calling + Character = Authenticity, etc.) with an animated equation reveal. A summary radar chart builds as participants score each dimension.
config.rationale: The hexagonal structure with progressive reveal mirrors the actual logical structure of the model — you cannot reach the integrated virtues without first engaging the foundational dimensions — making the architecture of the concept visible rather than merely stated.

blocks.title: Three Methods of Self-Discovery: Rank and Reflect

config.description: A drag-and-drop prioritization widget presenting three cards labeled 'Personal Reflection,' 'Feedback from Others,' and 'Formal Evaluation.' Participants first drag them into their personal order of preference (most comfortable to least). The widget then reveals DVULI's position — that all three are necessary and none is complete by itself — and displays a quote for each method. Participants are then prompted to click the method they have used least in the past year and type one reason why, generating a personal commitment to the upcoming formal assessments (DISC and LSA).
config.rationale: Asking participants to predict and rank before receiving instruction triggers retrieval practice of prior beliefs, creating productive cognitive conflict when the DVULI framework reveals that their least-used method is often their most needed one.
